question: Finnish parent (Oy Pohjoinen Ab) 100% owns Austrian subsidiary (Österreich GmbH). 2025 dividend of 600,000 euros to Finnish parent. Held 100% for three consecutive years. (a) Under Finland-Austria double tax treaty, at what withholding rate is the dividend taxed in Austria? (b) How much does Finnish parent actually receive after Austrian withholding? (c) Under Finnish participation exemption (osinkoverovapaus) rules applicable to corporate shareholders with significant holdings, is the received dividend taxable again in Finland?
gold_answer: (a) Under Article 10(3) of the Finland–Austria income- and capital-tax treaty, when the recipient of the dividend is a company resident in the other contracting state that directly controls (välittömästi hallitsee) at least 10% of the voting power (äänimäärä) of the company paying the dividend, the dividend may NOT be taxed in the source state. Oy Pohjoinen Ab owns 100% of Österreich GmbH, so the Austrian withholding tax under the treaty is 0% (the general 10% maximum in Article 10(2) does not apply because the 10(3) direct-holding exception overrides it). (b) Because the treaty rate is 0%, after applying the treaty Oy Pohjoinen Ab receives the full 600,000 e dividend without Austrian withholding. (Austria may initially apply its domestic withholding under Article 28; any amount exceeding the treaty rate is then refunded on application.) (c) Article 23(2)(b) of the same treaty provides: "Osinko, jonka Itävallassa asuva yhtiö maksaa Suomessa asuvalle yhtiölle, vapautetaan Suomen verosta, jos saaja välittömästi hallitsee vähintään 10 prosenttia osingon maksavan yhtiön äänimäärästä." Because Oy Pohjoinen Ab directly controls 100% (≥ 10%) of the voting power of Österreich GmbH, the dividend is exempt from Finnish tax under the treaty itself, in line with the Finnish participation-exemption regime for inter-company dividends from EEA-resident subsidiaries with sufficient holdings. The dividend is therefore not taxable again in Finland.

(b) Because the treaty rate is 0%, after applying the treaty Oy Pohjoinen Ab receives the full 600,000 e dividend without Austrian withholding.

question: Tommi platform primary account holder. Over six months in 2025, brother Juhani (no tax card) regularly works his shifts. Tommi receives platform's total compensation 60,000 euros and pays Juhani 25,000 euros. (a) Whose income is the entire 60,000 euros for tax reporting purposes, and within how many calendar days must it be reported to tulorekisteri? (b) What withholding rate must Tommi apply when paying Juhani (no tax card)? (c) Can Tommi deduct the 25,000 euros paid to Juhani as a work-related expense? (d) Is Tommi liable for employer's health insurance contributions on Juhani's payments?
gold_answer: (a) According to the Tax Administration's position paper on food couriers and their substitutes, when the courier (the primary account holder, here Tommi) has agreed with the platform / invoicing-service company that compensation is paid to him as työkorvaus (work-trade compensation), the entire payment from the courier-service platform is taxable earned income (work compensation) of the primary account holder, regardless of who actually performed the work: "koko suoritus on verotuksessa aina ruokalähetin (pääkäyttäjän) veronalaista ansiotuloa (työkorvausta)." So the full 60,000 e is Tommi's income for tax purposes. Reporting to tulorekisteri must be made by the payer no later than the fifth (5th) calendar day after the payment date ("viimeistään viidentenä kalenteripäivänä maksupäivän jälkeen"). (b) When Tommi pays Juhani for work performed as substitute, the rate depends on the nature of the relationship between Tommi and Juhani. In both cases — toimeksianto (work-trade) and työsuhde (employment) — if the recipient (Juhani) does not present a tax card, the payer must withhold 60% ("Jos sijainen ei ole esittänyt verokorttia, ruokalähetin on toimitettava ennakonpidätys 60 prosentin suuruisena."). So Tommi must apply a 60% withholding rate to Juhani's pay. (c) Yes. The position paper states explicitly that when the courier (private individual receiving työkorvaus) pays a substitute, he may deduct in his own taxation, as tulonhankkimiskulu (income-acquisition expense), either the wage paid to the substitute including its side costs, or the work compensation paid to the substitute: "ruokalähetti voi vähentää verotuksessaan tulohankkimiskuluina joko sijaiselle maksamansa palkan sivukuluineen tai sijaiselle maksamansa työkorvauksen määrän." So Tommi can deduct the 25,000 e he paid to Juhani. (d) Only if the relationship between Tommi and Juhani is an employment relationship (työsuhde): "Jos ruokalähetti on sopinut sijaisen kanssa työsuhteesta, ruokalähetin on … maksettava työnantajan sairausvakuutusmaksu." If Tommi and Juhani agreed on a toimeksiantosuhde (work-trade relationship), Tommi must only withhold and report the työkorvaus to tulorekisteri and is NOT liable for the employer's health insurance contribution. The facts (Juhani "regularly works his shifts" using Tommi's account, Tommi pays him directly) point typically toward toimeksianto unless there is an actual employment contract; in that case there is no employer's sairausvakuutusmaksu liability.
gold_key_facts: In both cases — toimeksianto (work-trade) and työsuhde (employment) — if the recipient (Juhani) does not present a tax card, the payer must withhold 60% ("Jos sijainen ei ole esittänyt verokorttia, ruokalähetin on toimitettava ennakonpidätys 60 prosentin suuruisena.").
So Tommi must apply a 60% withholding rate to Juhani's pay.

question: A Finnish employer obtained key personnel (avainhenkilö) tax status for a foreign specialist (Dr. Chen) in Jan 2022. He has been taxed at 32% withholding since then. In Jan 2026 the 48-month tax card expires. Payroll fails to notice and continues 32% withholding for Jan-Apr 2026. In May 2026 the tax authority's system flags it. (a) What standard progressive withholding rate should have applied from Jan 2026 (identify the applicable income bracket for 90,000 euros/yr)? (b) What is the procedure for correcting the under-withholding for those four months? (c) Can Dr. Chen apply for a new key personnel tax card if he has by now acquired Finnish tax residency?
gold_answer: (a) Once Dr. Chen's avainhenkilö tax card expired, his salary should be taxed under the ordinary progressive system (verotusmenettelylaki). Under the 2026 Verohallinnon päätös ennakonpidätysprosenttien laskentaperusteista palkkatuloa varten, the state additional-percentage scale (lisäprosenttiasteikko) places income above 52,100 euros in the top bracket at 40% state tax; a 90,000 euro annual salary therefore falls in the highest bracket (52,100.01 euros and above). The actual withholding percentage is the asteikko's 40% plus the employee's municipal tax, possible church tax, sairaanhoitomaksu (1.10%) and päivärahamaksu (0.88%) - in practice the additional-percentage rate for a 90,000 euro earner ends up around 55-58% depending on kunta. (b) Because liability for withholding the correct amount lies with the payer (avainhenkilölain 8 § / EPL 9 § 1 mom.), responsibility for the under-withheld tax is in the first instance on the employer; however, tax that has not been withheld can also be assessed on the employee (avainhenkilölain 9 §). When the tax authority detects the error, the situation is corrected through the lähdeveron määräämismenettely / verotuksen oikaisu (verotusmenettelylaki 56 § oikaisuajat). The employer must request a regular VML tax card for Dr. Chen (he applies for one in OmaVero), withholding switches to the progressive ennakonpidätys-/lisäprosentti, and the missing tax for Jan-Apr 2026 is collected either as additional withholding from later payments or as ennakkoveron / jäännösveron through Dr. Chen's regular tax assessment, with possible veronkorotus and viivästyskorko. (c) No - the avainhenkilö regime requires that the person was not Suomessa yleisesti verovelvollinen during the five calendar years preceding the start of the new work, and the law applies from the moment work begins. Once the original 48-month (now max 84-month after the 2024 amendment) period ends and Dr. Chen has been a Finnish tax resident for years, he no longer qualifies for a fresh key personnel tax card. The guidance is explicit that 'jos rajoitetusti verovelvolliseksi tullut henkilö palaa myöhemmin työskentelemään Suomeen, hänellä ei ole oikeutta avainhenkilöverotukseen, jos hän on ollut [...] Suomessa yleisesti verovelvollinen jonain hetkenä työskentelyn alkamisvuotta edeltäneen viiden vuoden aikana.' Dr. Chen is therefore taxed under ordinary progressive rules going forward.
gold_key_facts: The actual withholding percentage is the asteikko's 40% plus the employee's municipal tax, possible church tax, sairaanhoitomaksu (1.10%) and päivärahamaksu (0.88%) - in practice the additional-percentage rate for a 90,000 euro earner ends up around 55-58% depending on kunta.

(c) What is the deadline for submitting perukirja to the tax authority, and what documents must accompany it?
gold_answer: (a) Joonas inherits in 1. veroluokka (the first tax class). Perintö- ja lahjaverolaki 11 § places in the first class the perinnönjättäjän aviopuoliso, suoraan ylenevässä tai alenevassa polvessa oleva perillinen (i.e. direct descendants), aviopuolison suoraan alenevassa polvessa oleva perillinen, kihlakumppani and avopuoliso (only up to the PK 8:2 avustus). The fact that Markku and Helena were merely cohabitants (avoliitto) is irrelevant for Joonas - he is Markku's biological son in suoraan alenevassa polvessa, so I veroluokka applies regardless of whether Markku had ever been married to the son's mother. (b) Yes. Under PerVL 9 § 3 mom. and Verohallinto's guidance Hallintaoikeusvähennys perintöverotuksessa, when an heir receives property burdened by another person's lifelong hallintaoikeus, the value of that hallintaoikeus is deducted from the taxable inheritance. Helena is an avopuoliso with hallintaoikeustestamentti (hallintaoikeustestamentti can be granted to others such as an avopuoliso or child - 'Hallintaoikeustestamentti voidaan tehdä myös muille henkilöille, esimerkiksi avopuolisolle tai lapselle'). The hallintaoikeus value is calculated with the formula: oikeudenhaltijan ikäkerroin x omaisuuden käypä arvo x tuottokerroin. For an asunto/asuinkäytössä oleva kiinteistö the tuottokerroin is 5%; for a vapaa-ajan asunto it is 3%. Helena's age determines the ikäkerroin (e.g. age 59 -> 9, 70 -> 7, 73 -> 6, 80 -> 5, 83 -> 4 according to the examples in the ohje). The example matching Helena's circumstances (Esimerkki 22 in the ohje: a 59-vuotias avopuoliso whose hallintaoikeus to a 150,000 euro property gives a 67,500 euro deduction = 150,000 x 5% x ikäkerroin 9) shows the calculation. The deduction is capped at the value of the jäämistön säästö (Hallintaoikeusvähennyksen laskeminen asunnon käyvästä arvosta -kannanotto): 'Lesken hallintaoikeuden arvo lasketaan asunnon käyvästä arvosta, vaikka asunnon käypä arvo olisi jäämistön säästöä suurempi. Hallintaoikeuden arvona voidaan kuitenkin vähentää enintään jäämistön säästöä vastaava määrä.' Because Markku and Helena were not married, there is no laskennallinen ositus/puolesta määrin -periaate; the deduction applies to Markku's owned share of the apartment in full. (c) Perunkirjoitus (the perukirja meeting) must be held within 3 kuukautta of the death, and the perukirja itself must be sent to the Verohallinto within 1 kuukausi of the perunkirjoitus (Henkilöasiakkaat - Perunkirjoitus ja perukirja). Lisäaikaa must be applied for within 3 kuukautta from the death. The perukirja must list: vainajan ja lesken/avopuolison varat ja velat kuolinpäivänä, all kuolinpesän osakkaat (perilliset, leski, yleistestamentinsaajat) with henkilötunnukset and addresses, the kuolinpesän ilmoittaja, two uskottua miestä's signatures, sukulaisuussuhteet (and for Helena as testamentinsaaja the avopuoliso-status particulars - whether they had a yhteinen lapsi or were aiemmin avioliitossa), avio-oikeus tilanne, mahdolliset ennakkoperinnöt and the perinnönjättäjältä viimeisten 3 vuoden aikana saadut lahjat, vakuutuskorvaukset, hallintaoikeusvaatimus (Helena's vetoaminen testamentin hallintaoikeuteen). The required liitteet are testamentti (Markku's will), avioehtosopimus if any, perinnönjako- tai osituskirja jos jo tehty, sukuselvitys/virkatodistus, and a possible valtakirja for kuolinpesän veroasioiden hoitoa varten (lomake 3630). Vain kopiot toimitetaan Verohallintoon.
gold_key_facts: For an asunto/asuinkäytössä oleva kiinteistö the tuottokerroin is 5%; for a vapaa-ajan asunto it is 3%.

The example matching Helena's circumstances (Esimerkki 22 in the ohje: a 59-vuotias avopuoliso whose hallintaoikeus to a 150,000 euro property gives a 67,500 euro deduction = 150,000 x 5% x ikäkerroin 9) shows the calculation.

answer_result.question: Helena and Markku cohabited 15 years without marrying. Markku dies Feb 2026. They jointly owned an apartment. Markku's will: adult son Joonas (32, from previous relationship) sole heir, but Helena gets lifelong right of habitation (hallintaoikeus) over the apartment. (a) Under Finnish inheritance tax rules, what veroluokka applies to Joonas (Markku's son from previous relationship)? (b) Does Helena's lifelong right of habitation reduce taxable value for Joonas, and how is hallintaoikeuden arvo calculated relative to apartment's fair market value? (c) What is the deadline for submitting perukirja to the tax authority, and what documents must accompany it?
answer_result.answer:
(a) Joonas, as Markku’s son from a previous relationship, belongs to *veroluokka* I under the Finnish *Perintö- ja lahjaverolaki* (PerVL). The sources do not explicitly list the tax classes, but PerVL § 11 defines *veroluokka* I to include the deceased’s direct descendants, which covers Joonas.

(b) Yes, Helena’s lifelong right of habitation (*hallintaoikeus*) reduces the taxable value of the apartment for Joonas. Under PerVL § 9(3), the value of an obligation that requires the recipient to allow another person to use the property without consideration is deducted from the taxable value of the inheritance [Source 1]. The *hallintaoikeuden arvo* is calculated as a deduction from the fair market value of the apartment; the deduction is based on the capitalised value of the right, which depends on the age of the right-holder and the annual yield of the property [Source 1]. The sources do not provide the exact formula, but Vero’s guidance confirms that the deduction is made from the *käypä arvo* (fair market value) of the property [Source 1].

(c) The *perukirja* must be submitted to the *perintöverolautakunta* within one month of the *perunkirjoitus* (inventory of the estate) [Source 3]. It must be submitted in three copies [Source 3]. The following documents must accompany it: a copy of the *perinnönjakokirja* (deed of distribution) if the estate has been distributed [Source 3][Source 6]; a copy of the *osituskirja* (deed of partition) or *jakokirja* if a partition or distribution has been carried out between the deceased’s and a predeceased spouse’s successors [Source 6]; and a statement on whether the surviving spouse is exercising the right under *perintökaari* 3:1a to retain possession of the deceased spouse’s estate [Source 6]. Additionally, the *perukirja* must include the personal identity codes of the deceased and their successors [Source 6].

Huomioitavaa: [Source 2] is an older version of PerVL § 33 that requires two copies and submission to the *perintöverolautakunnan puheenjohtaja*. [Source 3] is the current version, requiring three copies and submission to the *perintöverolautakunta* itself. [Source 2] should not be relied upon.

question: A Finnish IT company employs Nguyen Van An (Vietnamese national) under secondment to work in Vietnam for 20 months from Jun 2025. Legal counsel applies for an A1 via paper form on the company's behalf. (a) What must the written power of attorney (valtakirja) contain to be valid for this paper application? (b) Once ETK issues the A1, to which three categories of authority does it automatically forward the decision? (c) Under the Finland-Vietnam double tax treaty, in which country is employment income taxable, and from what point in the secondment does Vietnam typically gain taxing rights?
gold_answer: (a) ETK does not provide a ready-made valtakirja template. According to the telp ohje 'Valtuuttaminen ja tiedonsaantioikeus ulkomaantyöskentelyn vakuuttamisasioissa', a paper-application valtakirja must contain at minimum: the valtuuttaja and the valtuutettu yksilöitynä (clearly identified), and the valtakirja must be allekirjoitettu. The valtakirja can be a yleinen, avoin valtakirja or a yksilöity valtakirja. It is additionally recommended (suositeltavaa) to specify the käyttötarkoitus (purpose) and voimassaoloaika (validity period). The customer drafts the valtakirja itself and submits it to ETK. (Note: Suomi.fi-valtuus is the primary channel for an A1 sähköinen hakemus; a separate kirjallinen valtakirja is needed only for paperilla tehtävä hakemus.) (b) Once ETK issues a decision on a Finnish ulkomaantyöskentelyn vakuuttaminen application (such as an A1), it automatically (automaattisesti) forwards the decision to: (1) Kela, (2) the työeläkelaitos (the relevant earnings-related pension provider), and (3) the ulkomaan sosiaaliturvaviranomainen (the foreign social-security authority of the country of secondment). Other Finnish viranomaiset have a statutory right to receive the same information on request. (c) The Finland-Vietnam double-tax treaty (Helsinki 21.11.2001) was brought into force in Finland by the Laki Vietnamin kanssa tuloveroja koskevan kaksinkertaisen verotuksen välttämiseksi ja veron kiertämisen estämiseksi tehdyn sopimuksen lainsäädännön alaan kuuluvien määräysten voimaansaattamisesta. The corpus contains only the implementing law, not the article-level treaty text, so the precise wording of the employment-income article (typically Article 15) and the day-count threshold are not directly verifiable in this corpus. Following the standard OECD-model 'employment income' rule that the treaty mirrors, employment income from work physically performed in Vietnam is in principle taxable in Vietnam (the state of work) once any of the standard conditions are not met - in particular, once the employee spends more than 183 days in Vietnam during the relevant 12-month/calendar-year period (a threshold that a 20-month secondment will cross quickly), or where the salary is borne by the Vietnamese permanent establishment of the employer, Vietnam gains primary taxing rights over the income earned there. Finland (state of residence at the start of work) typically retains taxing rights only for the part of the work that does not satisfy the >183-day / PE-borne tests; for a 20-month continuous stay the >183-day threshold is met and Vietnam's right applies from the point at which the 183-day count is reached, with double taxation eliminated under the treaty's elimination article (typically credit method for Finland).
gold_key_facts: (a) ETK does not provide a ready-made valtakirja template.

According to the telp ohje 'Valtuuttaminen ja tiedonsaantioikeus ulkomaantyöskentelyn vakuuttamisasioissa', a paper-application valtakirja must contain at minimum: the valtuuttaja and the valtuutettu yksilöitynä (clearly identified), and the valtakirja must be allekirjoitettu.
The valtakirja can be a yleinen, avoin valtakirja or a yksilöity valtakirja.

question: Maria runs a restaurant business as a sole trader (toiminimi) and filed quarterly VAT. From 2022-2024 she systematically misclassified restaurant sales (13.5% rate? - verify) as consulting (standard rate) to under-report. A 2026 audit uncovers the scheme. Undeclared VAT 48,000 euros. (a) For which tax years can the tax authority still reassess Maria's VAT obligation? (b) What is the correct VAT rate for restaurant and meal services in Finland (per the corpus - verify the rate quoted)? (c) Under provisions discussed in the Lotto winnings guidance regarding tax fraud, what are the potential criminal-law consequences for systematic under-reporting of this scale - specifically the maximum prison term and the applicable statute of limitations?
gold_answer: (a) Under the standard oma-aloitteisen verotuksen rules (oma-aloitteisten verojen verotusmenettelystä annettu laki, OVML), VAT can be reassessed within three calendar years from the start of the year following the tilikauden päättyminen / verokauden päättyminen. From a 2026-audit perspective the ordinary three-year window therefore reaches back to verokaudet whose tilikausi ended on or after 1.1.2023 - i.e. tilikaudet 2023, 2024 and 2025, plus any verokaudet of 2026 themselves. Importantly, however, the Lotto- ja muiden arpajaisvoittojen jakaminen kannanotto explicitly notes that 'määräaika verotuksen oikaisulle verovelvollisen vahingoksi on tavanomaista pidempi rikosperusteisessa verotuksen oikaisussa. Verohallinto voi toimittaa verotuksen tai oikaista verotusta verovelvollisen vahingoksi vielä vuoden kuluessa sen kalenterivuoden päättymisestä, jonka aikana syyttäjä on nostanut syytteen veropetosasiassa.' Because Maria's systematic misclassification across 2022-2024 is likely to meet the veropetos / törkeä veropetos threshold, this rikosperusteinen oikaisu lets Verohallinto reassess earlier years (including 2022) until one year after the kalenterivuosi during which a syyttäjä nostaa veropetossyytteen. (b) The correct Finnish VAT rate for ravintola- ja ateriapalvelut is 13.5% (alennettu verokanta). The corpus's Henkilöstöruokailun arvonlisäverotuksesta -ohje confirms this: 'oman käytön verotuksessa sovelletaan samoja verokantoja kuin myynnissä eli ravintola- ja ateriapalvelujen oman käytön verotuksessa sovellettava verokanta on alennettu 13,5 prosentin verokanta', and the worked examples consistently use 'ALV 13,5 % ravintolapalvelusta'. Misclassifying restaurant sales as consulting (yleinen 25,5% / standard rate as of 2025/2026) therefore over-reports rather than under-reports VAT at the line level, but if Maria was actually under-reporting the gross volume of sales by routing them to a different category that triggered different filings, the underlying liability is recomputed at 13.5% on the restaurant turnover. (c) Under the criminal-law section of the Lotto- ja muiden arpajaisvoittojen jakaminen kannanotto, systematic under-reporting of tax of this scale 'voi täyttää törkeän veropetoksen tunnusmerkistön, josta voidaan tuomita vankeuteen enintään neljäksi vuodeksi'. The kannanotto further states: 'esimerkiksi törkeän veropetoksen syyteoikeus vanhenee kymmenessä vuodessa.' So the maximum prison term is 4 years for törkeä veropetos and the syyteoikeus (statute of limitations on prosecution) is 10 years. Verohallinto must file the rikosilmoitus when the case meets the törkeän veropetoksen tunnusmerkistö ('Jos kysymyksessä olisi törkeä veropetos, rikosilmoitus on aina tehtävä.').
gold_key_facts: (b) The correct Finnish VAT rate for ravintola- ja ateriapalvelut is 13.5% (alennettu verokanta).

answer_result.question: Maria runs a restaurant business as a sole trader (toiminimi) and filed quarterly VAT. From 2022-2024 she systematically misclassified restaurant sales (13.5% rate? - verify) as consulting (standard rate) to under-report. A 2026 audit uncovers the scheme. Undeclared VAT 48,000 euros. (a) For which tax years can the tax authority still reassess Maria's VAT obligation? (b) What is the correct VAT rate for restaurant and meal services in Finland (per the corpus - verify the rate quoted)? (c) Under provisions discussed in the Lotto winnings guidance regarding tax fraud, what are the potential criminal-law consequences for systematic under-reporting of this scale - specifically the maximum prison term and the applicable statute of limitations?
answer_result.answer:
(a) The sources provided do not contain the reassessment periods for VAT obligations, so I cannot answer for which tax years the tax authority can reassess Maria’s VAT.

(b) The sources provided do not contain the current VAT rate for restaurant and meal services. [Source 8] discusses the VAT rate for foodstuffs and catering under the *arvonlisäverolaki* as it stood in 2013, stating that the tax on the sale of foodstuffs was 17% and that the reduced rate did not apply to catering (*tarjoilutoiminta*) [Source 8, kappale 17]. However, this rate is historical and the source does not state the rate applicable during 2022–2024.

(c) For systematic under-reporting of this scale, the applicable criminal provision is *veropetos* (tax fraud) under Chapter 29, Section 1 of the *rikoslaki* [Source 4]. The act criminalises, among other things, giving a false statement to the tax authority that affects the assessment of tax [Source 4, 1 momentti, kohta 1]. The maximum penalty for *veropetos* is imprisonment for at most two years [Source 4, 1 momentti]. Because the most severe punishment is over one year and at most two years of imprisonment, the statute of limitations for the right to prosecute is five years [Source 5, 2 momentti, kohta 3].

question: A syndicate of six Finnish friends has been buying lottery tickets together since 2022. Only Sirkka is registered as the player. In December 2025 the syndicate wins 3,000,000 euros. Sirkka wants to distribute 500,000 euros to each of the five other members per a pre-draw agreement. (a) Is Sirkka's own 500,000-euro share tax-free? (b) Are the other five members' shares tax-free, partially taxable, or fully taxable - under what income category? (c) Does the 7,500-euro gift tax threshold affect any of the five 500,000-euro transfers? (d) Each recipient's deadline for filing the relevant tax return after receiving their share? (e) Potential criminal-law consequences if the transfers are not reported?
gold_answer: (a) Yes. Under TVL 85 § the arpajaisvoitto from a Veikkaus-toimeenpanema rahapeli is verovapaata tuloa for the tunnistautunut and registered player. Sirkka is the only registered, tunnistautunut pelaaja, so her own keeping share of the 3,000,000 euro voitto - including the 500,000 euros she retains - is verovapaa arpajaisvoitto. (b) The five other syndicate members' shares are NOT tax-free. The Lotto- ja muiden arpajaisvoittojen jakaminen kannanotto is explicit that 'tuloverolain 85 §:n mukaisena verovapaana arpajaisvoittona voidaan pitää vain tunnistautuneelle pelaajalle maksettua arpajaisvoittoa' and that 'jos voiton lunastanut henkilö jakaa yksin saamansa arpajaisvoiton toisten henkilöiden kanssa ennen arvontaa tehdyn yhdessä pelaamista koskevan sopimuksen perusteella, arpajaisvoitto on verovapaa vain tunnistautuneelle ja voiton lunastaneelle pelaajalle ja muiden sopimuksen osapuolten saama tulo katsotaan muuksi veronalaiseksi ansiotuloksi.' Each non-registered syndicate member's 500,000 euros is therefore fully taxable as muu veronalainen ansiotulo under Sirkka-> recipient sharing pursuant to the pre-draw agreement (not as gift). (c) The 7,500-euro lahjavero threshold does NOT apply to these five transfers. Because the underlying voitonjakosopimus was made before the arvonta and because TVL 85 § verovapaus is restricted to the tunnistautunut pelaaja, the kannanotto categorises the others' shares as veronalaista ansiotuloa rather than lahjana. (Only if the voitonjakosopimus had been made AFTER the arvonta - i.e. Sirkka choosing to give some of her tax-free voitto to others - would PerVL 18 § lahja apply, and then the 1.1.2026 verotettavan lahjan alaraja 7,500 euroa would matter. Note: the 7,500 euro threshold replaced the old 5,000 euro threshold from 1.1.2026.) (d) Because each 500,000-euro share to a non-registered member is muuta veronalaista ansiotuloa rather than a lahja, the relevant return is the 2025 esitäytetty veroilmoitus for verovuosi 2025 (the year the win was paid). Recipients must report the income on the esitäytetty veroilmoitus by the standard spring 2026 deadline (typically May 2026 with the date marked on each individual's ilmoitus). For comparison, in the alternative gift-treatment scenario (which does not apply here), the lahjaveroilmoitus would have to be filed within 3 kuukautta of receiving the lahja. (e) If the recipients (or Sirkka) fail to report the 500,000-euro shares as veronalaista ansiotuloa, the kannanotto warns that 'lahjaveroilmoituksen laiminlyönti voi täyttää myös verorikoksen tunnusmerkistön. Verorikoksesta voi olla kyse myös, jos lahjansaaja tai -antaja veroa välttääkseen antaa veroviranomaiselle vääriä tietoja.' At the 500,000-euro scale per recipient (and 2.5 million euros aggregate), this 'voi täyttää törkeän veropetoksen tunnusmerkistön, josta voidaan tuomita vankeuteen enintään neljäksi vuodeksi'. Verohallinto must file a rikosilmoitus in törkeä veropetos cases and the syyteoikeus expires only after 10 years. In addition, Verohallinto can impose veronkorotus on top of the back-tax, and the rikosperusteinen oikaisu lets it reassess up to 1 year after the kalenterivuosi during which veropetossyyte was filed.
gold_key_facts: Sirkka is the only registered, tunnistautunut pelaaja, so her own keeping share of the 3,000,000 euro voitto - including the 500,000 euros she retains - is verovapaa arpajaisvoitto.
Sirkka choosing to give some of her tax-free voitto to others - would PerVL 18 § lahja apply, and then the 1.1.2026 verotettavan lahjan alaraja 7,500 euroa would matter.

answer_result.question: A syndicate of six Finnish friends has been buying lottery tickets together since 2022. Only Sirkka is registered as the player. In December 2025 the syndicate wins 3,000,000 euros. Sirkka wants to distribute 500,000 euros to each of the five other members per a pre-draw agreement. (a) Is Sirkka's own 500,000-euro share tax-free? (b) Are the other five members' shares tax-free, partially taxable, or fully taxable - under what income category? (c) Does the 7,500-euro gift tax threshold affect any of the five 500,000-euro transfers? (d) Each recipient's deadline for filing the relevant tax return after receiving their share? (e) Potential criminal-law consequences if the transfers are not reported?
answer_result.answer:
(a) Sirkka’s own 500,000-euro share is tax-free. Under *Tuloverolaki* (TVL) 85 §, a lottery win from *Veikkaus Oy* is tax-exempt for the identified player who purchased the ticket and to whom the win is paid [Source 8][Source 3]. Since Sirkka is the only registered player and the win was paid to her, her retained share qualifies as a tax-free *arpajaisvoitto*.

(b) The other five members’ shares are fully taxable as *muu veronalainen ansiotulo* (other taxable earned income). When a registered player distributes a win to others based on a pre-draw agreement to play together, the win is tax-free only for the identified player who redeemed it; the amounts received by the other parties to the agreement are treated as taxable earned income, not as a gift [Source 2].

(c) The 7,500-euro gift tax threshold does not affect the five 500,000-euro transfers because the transfers are not gifts. The *Verohallinto* guidance explicitly states that distributions under a pre-draw syndicate agreement constitute taxable income for the recipients, not a *lahja* under *Perintö- ja lahjaverolaki* 18 § [Source 2]. The gift rules, including the 7,500-euro threshold and the three-year aggregation rule, apply only when a winner gives away a win after receiving it without a pre-existing sharing agreement [Source 4].

(d) The sources do not specify a separate filing deadline for the recipients of this type of taxable earned income. The three-month deadline mentioned in the sources applies only to gift tax returns when a taxable gift is received [Source 4]. For earned income, the normal personal tax return schedule would apply, but the sources provide no further detail.

(e) The sources do not address potential criminal-law consequences for failing to report the transfers.
assumptions: Query expanded with Finnish keywords: arpajaisvoitto, TVL 85 §, lahjaverotus, PerVL.

question: Finnish employer applied wrong collective agreement to pharmacy worker (Apteekki Oy) for three years (2022-2024), using wages from expired agreement. Worker on maternity leave 6 months in 2023 with incorrect pay calculated. Correct agreement is Apteekkien työntekijöiden työehtosopimus, designated Yleissitova. (a) What does Yleissitova status mean for Apteekki Oy, not member of employers' federation? (b) Worker's remedy for underpaid wages — to whom complain, typical time limit? (c) How are maternity leave pay corrections handled, and which authority oversees compliance with generally binding collective agreements?
gold_answer: (a) A työehtosopimus that has been confirmed yleissitova (generally binding) by the yleissitovuuden vahvistamislautakunta is binding on every employer in the sector under Työsopimuslaki 2:7, regardless of whether the employer is a member of an employers' federation. Apteekki Oy must therefore observe at minimum the wage and other employment terms of the Apteekkien työntekijöiden työehtosopimus on every employment contract within the agreement's scope - any work contract term in conflict is void and the TES term applies in its place (see Työehtosopimuslaki 6 §). (b) The worker can demand the unpaid wages directly from the employer, then take the dispute to the general district court (käräjäoikeus) since a non-organized employer's TES dispute follows ordinary civil-court procedure rather than the työtuomioistuin route reserved for parties bound to the TES. The general statute of limitation for wage claims is 5 years (palkkasaatavan vanhentumisaika 5 vuotta) under Työsopimuslaki 13:9. The worker may also contact the labour-protection authority (työsuojeluviranomainen / aluehallintovirasto / länsstyrelsen) for advice and supervision. (c) Maternity-leave salary that the employer paid wrongly under an expired TES must be recalculated based on the correct yleissitova TES rates that applied during the leave; the correction is paid as back-pay (jälkikäteinen palkkakorvaus) and the worker can claim it within the 5-year wage-claim limit. question: Eeva worked as tax consultant at large Finnish accounting firm 2019-2024. Advised clients to use 24% standard VAT rate instead of 13.5% restaurant rate for catering services. Some overpaid, others underpaid. Left firm early 2024. 2026 audit covers period 2020-2023. (a) From perspective of clients who overpaid VAT, what is the refund claim process and time limit? (b) From perspective of clients who underpaid VAT, what is tax authority's reassessment window? (c) Errors were professional negligence, not intentional fraud. How does this affect potential criminal liability? (d) For client who under-reported by largest amount (35,000 euros gap across three years), assuming criminal charges in late 2026, what is maximum prison term and statute of limitations?
gold_answer: (a) Restaurant and catering services (ravintola- ja ateriapalvelu) are taxable at the reduced VAT rate of 13.5%, not the 24% standard rate. Clients who overpaid VAT can claim a refund by submitting a corrective oma-aloitteisten verojen veroilmoitus to the Verohallinto. The general statutory window for changing self-assessed taxes (incl. VAT) is 3 years from the beginning of the year following the tax year/period (VML 55/56 §, OVML 44 §). For 2020 VAT periods, the 3-year limit had expired by the end of 2023; for 2021-2023 periods, claims could still be filed depending on when the overpayment was made. (b) The Verohallinto's reassessment window for changing VAT to the taxpayer's detriment is also 3 years (yleinen määräaika), counted from the start of the year following the tax period. By 2026 the 2020 and 2021 (and most of 2022) periods are out of time under the ordinary 3-year rule, but the period can be extended in cases involving rikos (crime): if the prosecutor brings a tax-fraud charge, the Verohallinto may still reassess up to one year after the end of the calendar year in which the charge was filed. (c) Pure professional negligence by the adviser is not, by itself, a criminal act. Criminal tax-fraud (veropetos) requires that the taxpayer (or representative) gave 'vääriä tietoja' to the tax authority to evade tax; mere unintentional error advised by a consultant typically results only in tax reassessment plus veronkorotus (administrative tax surcharge), not criminal charges. The Verohallinto may decide not to file a rikosilmoitus if it considers the veronkorotus a sufficient sanction, weighing seriousness, repetition, expected sentence, tax amount and surcharge. (d) For a 35,000 EUR underpayment spread over three years (i.e., not necessarily 'huomattavan suuri' in a single act), if charges were nevertheless brought as törkeä veropetos (aggravated tax fraud), the maximum prison term is 4 years and the syyteoikeus (right to prosecute) statute of limitations is 10 years. If treated as ordinary veropetos (not aggravated), the maximum prison term is shorter (under Rikoslaki 29:1, up to 2 years) and the prosecution time limit is 5 years - meaning charges in late 2026 would already be out of time for ordinary veropetos relating to 2020 errors but not for törkeä veropetos.
gold_key_facts: (a) Restaurant and catering services (ravintola- ja ateriapalvelu) are taxable at the reduced VAT rate of 13.5%, not the 24% standard rate.

question: A Finnish company applies the margin scheme (marginaaliverotusmenettely) when selling second-hand cars. In November 2025 it buys a used car for 12,000 euros and sells it for 15,500 euros. How is VAT calculated under the margin scheme?

In this case, the company buys a used car for 12,000 euros and sells it for 15,500 euros, so the margin is 3,500 euros (15,500 euros minus 12,000 euros). VAT is not calculated on the full selling price, but only on this margin. The applicable VAT rate for second-hand goods under the margin scheme is the standard rate, which is 25.5% as of November 2025. The VAT included in the margin is calculated by dividing the margin by 1.255 and then multiplying by 0.255, or more simply, by using the formula: VAT = margin × (VAT rate / (100% + VAT rate)). The company must report only the VAT calculated on the margin, not on the total sales price, in its VAT return.

question: A Finnish holding company receives dividends from its wholly owned subsidiaries and charges management fees to them. It incurs 600,000 euros of professional advisory fees partly related to acquiring a new subsidiary. Can the holding company recover any input VAT on these fees?

question: Under Finnish FAS (KPL 5:6), what does the lower of cost and net realisable value rule mean for inventory valuation and what must a company do when NRV falls below cost?
gold_answer: Under Finnish FAS, specifically section 5:6 of the Accounting Act (KPL 5:6), the lower of cost and net realisable value rule means that inventory must be valued on the balance sheet at the lower of its original acquisition cost or its net realisable value (NRV) at the balance sheet date. Net realisable value is defined as the estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business, less any estimated costs necessary to make the sale. If the NRV of inventory falls below its acquisition cost, the company is required to write down the value of the inventory to its NRV, recognising the loss as an expense in the income statement. question: In Finnish case law, how have the courts interpreted the concept of genuine business reasons (liiketaloudelliset perusteet) in the context of tax-neutral corporate restructurings under EVL 52h§?
gold_answer: In Finnish case law, the courts have interpreted the concept of genuine business reasons (liiketaloudelliset perusteet) in the context of tax-neutral corporate restructurings under EVL 52h§ as requiring that the restructuring is motivated by legitimate commercial objectives rather than tax avoidance. Genuine business reasons may include improving operational efficiency, facilitating business growth, separating different business activities, enhancing competitiveness, or responding to changes in market conditions. The courts have emphasised that restructurings must be planned and executed primarily for business purposes, such as streamlining management structures or preparing for expansion, and not solely to obtain tax benefits. If the restructuring is found to be driven mainly by tax motives, the tax-neutral treatment may be denied. The assessment is made on a case-by-case basis, considering the overall context and documentation provided by the company. answer_result.question: In Finnish case law, how have the courts interpreted the concept of genuine business reasons (liiketaloudelliset perusteet) in the context of tax-neutral corporate restructurings under EVL 52h§?
answer_result.answer:
The Finnish Supreme Administrative Court (*korkein hallinto-oikeus*, KHO) has interpreted the concept of genuine business reasons (*liiketaloudelliset perusteet*) in the context of tax-neutral corporate restructurings under *EVL* 52h § primarily through the lens of the provision's purpose and EU law, emphasizing a case-by-case, holistic assessment.

The legislative intent behind the specific anti-avoidance rule in *EVL* 52h § is to ensure that taxation does not hinder restructurings carried out for genuine business reasons, such as modifying corporate structures, reorganizing, or rationalizing operations, and it is not meant to prevent normal tax planning [Source 1]. The interpretation of this provision must also consider the EU Merger Directive and related case law, which requires national authorities to examine the transaction as a whole on a case-by-case basis, rather than applying predetermined general criteria [Source 2].

In practice, KHO has found that a restructuring was not motivated by genuine business reasons when it was undertaken in the interest of the parent group rather than the company itself, such as a "debt push down" arrangement designed to meet external financiers' requirements for loan collateral, which the court noted cannot have binding effect in taxation [Source 4]. Conversely, the court has ruled that the *EVL* 52h § anti-avoidance rule does not apply when a restructuring does not create concrete tax advantages that are alien to the tax system, even if one of the key purposes of the arrangement was the rapid realization of assets [Source 3]. The presence of a strong, tax-independent business reason, such as a restructuring's central cause being a compelling business rationale unrelated to tax, has also been a decisive factor against applying the general anti-avoidance rule in *VML* 28 § [Source 3].

question: The Finnish general anti-avoidance rule (veronkiertämissäännös) in VML 28§ can override technically lawful tax planning arrangements. What are the conditions for its application and how have the Finnish courts applied it?
gold_answer: The Finnish general anti-avoidance rule (veronkiertämissäännös) in VML 28§ allows the tax authorities to disregard the legal form of a transaction and recharacterise it for tax purposes if certain conditions are met, even if the arrangement is technically lawful. The key conditions for its application are that the arrangement must result in an unjustified tax benefit and that the main or one of the main purposes of the arrangement is to avoid tax. The rule applies when the legal structure of a transaction is artificial or does not correspond to its actual economic substance, and the arrangement is not motivated by genuine business reasons. Finnish courts have applied VML 28§ by examining the overall purpose and economic reality of the arrangement. If the courts find that the primary aim was to obtain a tax advantage without a legitimate commercial justification, they have upheld the tax authority’s right to disregard the arrangement and impose tax as if the arrangement had not been made. The courts assess factors such as the artificiality of the structure, the lack of real business purpose, and the presence of circular or contrived transactions. However, if the taxpayer can demonstrate genuine business reasons for the arrangement, the anti-avoidance rule is generally not applied. Each case is assessed individually, with a focus on substance over form and the intention behind the transaction.

VML 28 §:n soveltamisedellytykset jakautuvat kahteen päätyyppiin. Ensinnäkin säännöstä sovelletaan, kun olosuhteelle tai toimenpiteelle on annettu oikeudellinen muoto, joka ei vastaa asian varsinaista luonnetta tai tarkoitusta, eli kyse on yksityisoikeudellisen muodon ja tosiasiallisen taloudellisen sisällön ristiriidasta [Source 1]. Toiseksi säännös soveltuu, kun kauppahinta, vastike tai suoritusaika on määrätty tai toimenpiteeseen on ryhdytty ilmeisesti siinä tarkoituksessa, että suoritettavasta verosta vapauduttaisiin [Source 1]. Arviointi on tosiseikkoihin perustuvaa kokonaistarkastelua, jossa veroetu on lain tarkoitukselle vieras, toimi on epätavallinen, keinotekoinen ja ilman veroetua ilmeisen tarkoitukseton [Source 2].

Korkeimman hallinto-oikeuden ratkaisukäytännössä VML 28 §:ää on sovellettu monenlaisiin järjestelyihin. Tapauksessa KHO:2017:20 kommandiittiyhtiön voitonjakolainalle maksettava tuottokorko katsottiin veronalaiseksi elinkeinotuloksi, koska järjestelyyn oli ryhdytty ensi sijassa verotuksellisista syistä ja se oli poikkeuksellinen ja keinotekoinen [Source 5]. Tapauksessa KHO:2017:128 hallinto-oikeuden olisi tullut ratkaista VML 28 §:n soveltuminen mezzanine-lainojen korkovähennyksiin [Source 3]. KHO:2021:178:ssa todettiin, että velkojen korkojen vähennysoikeutta voidaan rajoittaa VML 28 §:n nojalla [Source 6]. KHO:2025:37 vahvisti, että yleistä veronkiertosäännöstä voidaan soveltaa yritysjärjestelyiden verotuksessa erityissäännöksen (EVL 52 h §) rinnalla [Source 8].

VML 28 §:n ja peitellyn osingon (VML 29 §) välinen suhde on toissijainen: ensin tutkitaan VML 29 §, ja jos se ei sovellu, tutkitaan VML 28 §, mutta säännöksiä voidaan soveltaa myös rinnakkain esimerkiksi bulvaanitilanteissa [Source 7]. question: Koivunen Retail Oy sells goods to a customer in the Åland Islands (Ahvenanmaa). How does the Åland tax border regime affect the VAT treatment of goods moving between mainland Finland and Åland?
gold_answer: When Koivunen Retail Oy sells goods to a customer in the Åland Islands (Ahvenanmaa), the Åland tax border regime affects the VAT treatment because Åland is considered a separate VAT area from mainland Finland under Finnish and EU law. gold_answer: When Saarinen Construction Oy purchases a new diesel van for 42,000 euros excluding VAT to be used entirely for business deliveries, Finnish car tax (autovero) does not apply to vans that are registered and used exclusively for business purposes, such as goods transport. Regarding VAT, if the van is used exclusively for VAT-taxable business activities, the company can fully recover the input VAT on the purchase.
